_meta.description: Material properties for construction-scale 3D printing. Covers concrete mixes, geopolymers, and earth-based materials used in structural printing. Agent-consumable for architectural design reasoning.

agent_guidance: Standard OPC concrete is the workhorse for structural printing. Proven in hundreds of buildings worldwide.
Interlayer bond is the critical weak point — 60% of bulk strength. Design for this, not bulk compressive strength.
Open time is CRITICAL. If the next layer isn't placed within 30 minutes, the bond fails. Plan toolpaths accordingly.
Layer height and width directly affect structural capacity. Thicker layers = fewer bonds = stronger wall. But slower prints.
Reinforcement (rebar, fiber, post-tension) is required for any structural element over 2.5m tall or spanning openings.
Foundation is always required. Structural printers print walls, not foundations. Traditional foundation precedes printing.
Weather sensitivity: rain stops printing. Wind > 20 mph affects layer placement accuracy. Temperature < 5C prevents curing.
28-day cure is standard. Structure can't take design load until cure completes. Plan occupancy timelines accordingly.
Cost advantage over traditional comes from speed and labor reduction, NOT material cost. Material cost is comparable or higher.
ICON's proprietary mix — optimized for their Titan printer system. Higher performance than standard concrete.
Better interlayer bond (2.0 MPa vs 1.5 MPa standard) means more forgiving print timing and taller walls per session.
Wider print speed range (100-500 mm/s) enables faster prints than standard concrete mixes.
31% lower embodied carbon vs CMU baseline per MIT CSHub — use this in sustainability-focused designs.
Higher material cost ($250/m3 vs $150/m3) but less waste (3% vs 5%) and faster printing offsets it.
Only available through ICON's Titan ecosystem. Cannot be sourced independently.
Longer open time (up to 90 min) gives more flexibility for complex toolpaths and multi-wall geometries.

compliance.certification_path: Performance-based per ACI 318 — no prescriptive path yet for geopolymers in AC509

agent_guidance: LOWEST CARBON FOOTPRINT — 80% less CO2 than standard Portland cement concrete. Leading sustainability option.
Uses industrial waste products (fly ash, slag) as the binder instead of Portland cement. Circular economy material.
Faster cure (7 days vs 28 days) — structures reach design strength 4x sooner. Major schedule advantage.
Higher fire resistance (6 hours vs 4 hours) — valuable for defense and commercial applications.
Shorter open time and pumpability — more demanding on print planning and equipment reliability.
No prescriptive code path yet for structural printing. Requires performance-based engineering per ACI 318.
Lower material cost ($120/m3) than standard concrete ($150/m3) when waste feedstock is locally available.
Higher minimum print temperature (10C) — not suitable for cold-weather printing without heated enclosures.

compliance.certification_path: Varies by jurisdiction — earth building codes exist in NM, NZ, AU

agent_guidance: CHEAPEST AND MOST SUSTAINABLE option — $30/m3, 95% less carbon than Portland cement. Uses local soil.
VERY LOW STRUCTURAL CAPACITY. Single-story only. Lightweight roof only (no concrete roof slabs).
Outstanding thermal mass — passive cooling in hot climates. R-value is modest but thermal mass handles diurnal swings.
Best for: developing world housing, disaster relief shelters, sustainability-focused single-story structures.
Not suitable for: multi-story, high-wind zones, seismic zones without significant engineering, cold climates.
Largest shrinkage of any print material (2%) — design for shrinkage control (expansion joints every 3-4 meters).
Longest open time (up to 2 hours) — most forgiving for toolpath complexity and equipment issues.
Requires moisture protection — earth walls need roof overhangs, lime plaster, or waterproof coating to survive rain.
Perfect for the humanitarian use case: local materials, minimal equipment, single-story dwellings, extremely low cost.
